name: Norse Mythology
definition: The Aesir are the primary pantheon of Norse gods associated with order, governance, and war - including Odin, Thor, and Frigg - distinguished from the Vanir (fertility gods) in Norse cosmology.
examples: Odin - Chief god of wisdom and war
Thor - Protector god with mighty hammer Mjolnir
Tyr - God of war and justice

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: From the primordial void of Ginnungagap, the worlds of fire (Muspelheim) and ice (Niflheim) met, creating the giant Ymir. The gods, led by Odin, slew Ymir and fashioned the world from his body.

keyMyths.title: The Binding of Fenrir
keyMyths.description: The monstrous wolf Fenrir, son of Loki, posed a great threat to the gods. They attempted to bind him with increasingly strong chains, only to be thwarted until the dwarves crafted Gleipnir, a seemingly weak but unbreakable chain.

keyMyths.title: Thor's Journey to Utgard
keyMyths.description: Thor, accompanied by Loki and Thialfi, journeyed to the fortress of Utgard, ruled by Utgarda-Loki. There, they faced seemingly impossible challenges that tested their strength, speed, and cunning, only to discover they were tricked by illusions.

keyMyths.title: The Death of Baldr
keyMyths.description: Baldr, the beloved son of Odin and Frigg, was invulnerable to all things except mistletoe. Loki tricked Hodr, Baldr's blind brother, into throwing a mistletoe dart, killing Baldr and plunging the gods into grief.

keyMyths.title: Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: Ragnarok is the prophesied end of the world, a cataclysmic battle between the gods and the forces of chaos. It involves the death of many gods, the destruction of Asgard, and the subsequent rebirth of a new world.
keyMyths.source: Poetic Edda, Völuspá; Prose Edda, Gylfaginning

sources.name: Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems primarily preserved in the Icelandic medieval manuscript Codex Regius.
sources.title: Poetic Edda

sources.name: Prose Edda
sources.description: Written by Snorri Sturluson in the 13th century, a handbook of Norse mythology and skaldic poetry.
sources.title: Prose Edda

sources.name: Gesta Danorum
sources.description: A patriotic work of Danish history, by Saxo Grammaticus, featuring euhemerized accounts of Norse gods and heroes.
sources.title: Gesta Danorum

sources.name: Archaeological Evidence
sources.description: Runestones, bracteates, and other artifacts provide material evidence of Norse beliefs and practices.
sources.title: Archaeological Evidence

sources.name: Skaldic Poetry
sources.description: A highly complex and stylized form of Old Norse poetry, often commissioned by kings and chieftains, that provides insights into Norse mythology and worldview.
sources.title: Skaldic Poetry
